summary: I spent a lot of my early musical career in Chicago Heights, Illinois. It was there that I played with local bands like the Rhythm-Aires and experienced some of the best jam sessions I've ever been part of. The musicians in Chicago Heights really knew how to play and groove together without showing off, unlike some sessions I attended in Chicago. Those sessions would start early in the afternoon and go on for hours, creating an incredible musical atmosphere.

Club Casanova was a significant place for me early on. The manager there heard my band one night and asked me to gather a few guys to play at the club. We had a four-piece group and played three shows a night for about twelve weeks. It was my first real steady gig and I had to adapt all my arrangements to the tenor scale, which was a new challenge for me.

I worked with Jimmy Reynolds in a ten-piece band after my stint at Club Casanova. During the summer, we took that band to Cedar Lake to play in a taxi dance hall, which was the toughest job I ever had. The band was demanding with only one short intermission, and we played our entire library every night. Despite the hard work, it was an important part of my growth as a musician.

Cedar Lake was the location of the taxi dance hall where I worked with Jimmy Reynolds' band during the summer. That job was incredibly tough, with only a four-minute intermission each night and a packed crowd until closing. We played nonstop and were exhausted by the end of each night. It was a real test of endurance and musicianship for me.

Bix Beiderbecke has been my biggest influence in music. I first really understood what swing was when I heard his records. Everyone who worked with him thought he was a great guy, even though I don't agree with some of his personal choices like smoking weed. Still, his playing was exceptional and I believe almost every musician today has been influenced by him in some way.

I liked listening to Tommy Dorsey a lot, even though I didn't hear many guitar solos until recently. His music was part of what shaped my appreciation for swing. Along with Benny Goodman, Dorsey’s style has remained consistently good over the years. Their music has a lasting quality that I admire.

Benny Goodman was one of the musicians I admired early on. Like Tommy Dorsey, his music never changed in quality from when I first heard it. Goodman’s swing style is timeless and has been a steady influence on me. I appreciate how his music has endured and continues to inspire.

We had some records of Andrés Segovia, the classical guitar player, and I think he's great. His playing showed me a different side of guitar music, more classical and refined. Although my focus is jazz, I respect Segovia’s artistry and the influence classical guitar can have on a musician’s development.

Coleman Hawkins is one of those musicians who never changed from when I first heard him. His music has a kind of lasting quality that I really respect. Hawkins, along with Goodman and Dorsey, represents a style that endures and continues to influence players like me. Their music is something I always come back to.

Louis Armstrong’s playing is different from mine and I never really benefited from it the way I did from others. His attack and expression are unique—jagged in delicacy but powerful in feeling. I think Armstrong tries to make great music rather than just good music, which sometimes means he isn't always perfect. Still, his impact on jazz is undeniable.

Johnny Hodges plays a different kind of music than I do, so I didn't really benefit from his style. Although I respect his musicianship, his approach didn't influence my playing the way others like Bix Beiderbecke or Goodman did. Hodges’ style is distinct and separate from my own musical path.

Teddy Wilson’s piano playing is delicate and refined. When he plays gut bucket style, it has a different feel, but when he plays lightly and politely, as Armstrong would say, he is the best. I appreciate his versatility and the subtlety he brings to jazz piano. His playing is something I admire and learn from.

I've been playing with the Deuces for about four months now. It's a great place to get into a groove, and with my quartet there, I can really get off on three good grooves a night. Before joining the Deuces, I had trouble finding that kind of musical connection with larger orchestras. The Deuces has given me a chance to really express myself musically.

I've played plenty of sessions in Chicago, but I never liked them as much as those in Chicago Heights. The Chicago sessions tend to be about showing off and taking as many choruses as possible, which kills the groove. In Chicago Heights, the musicians really played together and felt the music, which made all the difference. Chicago has a different vibe that just didn't suit me.

I first played with Jimmy McPartland at the Deuces and really liked his trumpet playing. He was part of the Dixieland music scene, which I had always admired but never played with musicians who really knew it until then. I even dreamed about him leading our orchestra one night, which shows how much influence he had on me. Jimmy’s style helped set me in a groove and inspired some of my compositions.

Raymond Scott’s music is different from jazz because it uses open description. For example, his piece Stormy Night Aboard An Ocean Liner really lets you see and feel the story through the music. I admire that approach and tried to do something similar with my own composition Howard Street Express, which captures the feeling of a crazy express train ride. Scott’s style has inspired me to think more about the imagery behind music.
cues: Stormy Night Aboard An Ocean Liner

entity: Stormy Night Aboard An Ocean Liner
summary: Stormy Night Aboard An Ocean Liner is a piece by Raymond Scott that really impressed me because it paints a vivid picture through music. You can almost see and feel the stormy ocean liner in the sounds. It inspired me to write my own descriptive piece, Howard Street Express, where I tried to capture the sensations of a wild train ride. That kind of musical storytelling is something I admire.

Howard Street Express is a piece I wrote to capture the feeling of riding the express train on Howard Street. I was in a terrific groove after a fight with my girl and that ride stayed on my mind for weeks. Unlike most of my music, I play this piece exactly the same every time because it represents a very specific experience. It’s my attempt to describe the sounds, thoughts, and feelings of that ride through music.

Scatter Brain Rag is a tune I wrote about three or four months ago, inspired by my first time playing at the Deuces. It’s a goofy title, but the piece resembles a march and reflects how Dixieland music connects with marches. Playing with Jimmy McPartland and others who really knew Dixieland set me in a groove that led to this composition. It’s a way of expressing my musical experiences at the Deuces.

Dixieland music has always been tops with me, but I hadn’t played with musicians who really knew it until I joined the Deuces and played with guys like Jimmy McPartland. We played three-four time Dixieland tunes that sounded like marches, which gave me new ideas about how Dixieland relates to other forms of music. That experience helped me write Scatter Brain Rag and deepened my appreciation for the style.
